old_claim.note: Legal Systems (Common/Civil Law) provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Medieval Italian notarial records developed in Roman-law-influenced legal systems where notaries authenticated private legal acts.

source_shape.source_locator: Article extract says areas influenced by Roman law used notaries and that medieval Italians developed notarial records as a powerful form of writing.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source links notarial records to Roman-law-influenced areas and medieval Italian notarial practice.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The legal-system prerequisite is justified by the Roman-law/civil-law context of medieval Italian notarial records.

ontology_before: Notarial records depended on legal systems through a generic expert-inference edge.
ontology_after: Medieval notarial registers keep legal_systems_common_civil as a source-backed legal institutional context.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Preserved: legal_systems_common_civil remains a direct enabling prerequisite, now source-backed.
would_reject_if: The node were scoped to common-law notaries rather than civil-law notarial registers.
The source only supported general archives without Roman-law or notarial context.
The prerequisite were interpreted as a claim that common-law and civil-law systems both caused the Genoese register tradition.
short_reason: Medieval Italian notarial records are source-linked to Roman-law-influenced legal systems.
